summary: Gruber reviews the newly released Google Maps app for iPhone, noting that three months after the iOS 6 mapping controversy, iPhone users now have a better Google Maps experience than they ever had with Apple's built-in Maps. He analyzes the app's design as clean and distinctly Google-branded rather than an Android port, while noting some navigational UI quirks. He observes that Google aggressively pushes users to sign in, which serves Google's data collection interests. In updates, he clarifies that the situation worked out for users despite not being Apple's plan, and that Apple is likely seething over the outcome. He also details several privacy concerns, including a deliberately tiny opt-out checkbox and a sign-in flow designed to imply it's required.
key_points: iPhone users ended up with a better Google Maps experience than they had before iOS 6, with vector tiles and turn-by-turn navigation — the controversy resolved in users' favor
Google's iOS app design is distinctly Google-branded rather than an Android port, and in fact looks more 'Google-y' than Android itself does
Google aggressively pushes sign-in and data collection, using dark patterns like unusually small opt-out checkboxes and a skip button designed to be overlooked
Apple is likely seething that Google Maps became a celebrated App Store release rather than Apple's own Maps being good enough to make users not miss Google
Gruber honestly acknowledges he's unqualified to judge mapping data quality since he rarely uses maps, separating design critique from service quality
money_quotes: Here we are three months after iOS 6 was released and iPhone users now have a better Google Maps experience than they did when Google was providing the back-end data for the built-in Maps app. It all worked out.
Android's look and feel match its name: sterile, robotic. Google's brand has always been very friendly, colorful, humane.
If anything, Google's iOS apps look and feel more Google-y, brand-wise, than Android does.
Fewer people will use Google Maps now that it's an App Store download instead of the back-end for the built-in Maps app, but those who do will be far more likely to engage with the Google ecosystem.
What makes it remarkable is that it's all worked out despite clearly not being Apple's plan.
I think Apple — where by 'Apple', I mean the company's collective executive leadership — is seething regarding the way this has played out.
We've got two apps with turn-by-turn-navigation and vector map tiles where before we had none.
This is designed to imply, without saying so, that you have to sign in to use the app.
key_insight: The iOS 6 maps debacle inadvertently produced the best outcome for users — two competing maps apps with modern features — while leaving both Apple (embarrassed) and Google (losing default status) worse off than before.
